reasoning: The historical average of 0.417 protests per 30 days in Aruba strongly suggests an underlying base rate of about 5 protests per year. Assuming a uniform Poisson process, the probability of observing at least one protest in any 30-day window is roughly 34% (1 - e^-0.417). For the first resolution date (June 29), we are already 23 days into the 30-day window, and extensive search of local and international news sources shows no credible reports of protests in Aruba. With only 7 days left in the window and a small chance that a minor unrecorded event already occurred, the probability is roughly 16%. For the three future windows entirely ahead of the forecast date (July 22, Sept 20, Dec 19), I anchor to the base rate of 34%. Political tensions have cooled following the relatively stable elections in December 2024, but random union or civic strikes remain possible. As the forecast horizon increases, the uncertainty constraint pulls the probabilities gradually toward 0.5, reflecting that distant economic or political shifts could increase or decrease the frequency of unrest.
